translation.content: When it comes to minimalist dance music, Ruben Üvez, AKA Konduku, is one of the best in the game right now. With a masterful and ever-shapeshifting understanding of rhythm, the Berlin-based artist crafts sublime dance music with a staunchly leftfield bent. Don't just take our word for it: how many DJs, after all, can claim to have moved De School to a puddle of tears? Musically, Üvez is hard to pin down. He's often billed as a techno artist, but actually you'll find his sound sits outside of the genre's many conventions. With an outsider's curiosity, he leans into diverse moods, tempos and genres, though one throughline is always how he arranges his drums. Whether it be deep, Nobu-core techno such as 2023's Hayal EP or UK beat science á la Peverelist on 2019's Gegek, he leads with rhythm across his DJing and production. The end product is a hypnotic, one-of-a-kind sound that hits the body before the brain has time to catch up. In short, it slaps. As Üvez's RA Podcast demonstrates, he's got a serious knack for crafting and selecting tunes that can deeply captivate a dance floor. Clocking in at just over 90 minutes, RA.967 is an excursion through a timeless sound, packed with long, layered blends, flick-of-the-wrist transitions, locked grooves and spine-tingling atmospherics. In 2020, we called Ruben Üvez one of techno's brightest new talents. This mix sees him ascending to a seat at the top table. – Bella Aquilina You've just come back from a tour in Japan. How was it? This was my fourth visit to Japan, and I also had the chance to visit Seoul for the first time. One of the highlights of the trip was playing for [DJ] Nobu's Future Terror party, a collaboration with Aurora [Halal's] festival, Sustain-Release. I've built a great relationship with Nobu recently, and we're about to release the second EP, Gazoz. It was exciting to have everything align—a gig in Japan, the EP release, and being part of such a special event. The biggest difference I've noticed between Japan and Europe is how people approach nightlife. In Japan, attendees are very intentional; it's more like a concert experience, especially at indoor events. People come specifically for you as an artist and are incredibly knowledgeable and dedicated, even though the scene is small. In Europe, by contrast, people often go out without a clear plan—they're there for the atmosphere rather than a particular artist. Japan's entertainment culture is diverse. You can find countless bars, karaoke spots and other activities, but electronic music remains a niche. That exclusivity makes it feel even more special and vibrant. Can you tell us how and where the mix was recorded, and share the idea behind it? I recorded it with my turntables and a Traktor scratch for the digital tracks. Sometimes I feel that I have quite a few sides to my DJing, so the idea was basically to combine my techno side with my tech house side. I wanted to have a coherent mix that I'll still enjoy listening to in five years. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? De School was the club with the biggest influence. I played there for the first time after I released my album in 2018. It was quite special, because it was the first big gig I did. Slowly, I got asked back again and again and again, and then became a resident. I learned a lot about DJing there, how to play opening slots to closing slots and everything in between. And also, I was surrounded by a lot of other artists and club regulars. We all gave each other a lot of inspiration. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Palestine, of course, is really an important issue that people should put more attention to. But also the rise of fascism, which is very scary. Palestine is also a part of this. And this is happening in the Netherlands, Germany, Turkey, everywhere around us. The majority of your releases have been with Nous'klaer Audio. How has the label shaped you as an artist? Quite a lot. Of course, I had my first release, Kıran, on there, which was special. There's a funny story behind that actually. My friend, Tammo [Hesselink] had a song that Sjoerd [Oberman] really wanted, and so Tammo said, you can release it if you put out ten tracks by Konduku. But then in the end, I started to send Sjoerd my tracks and we ended up with an album. It's special for my first release to be an album. The label has always given me a lot of space to do what I want, especially on the album. At some point we started to do this K series, which was club focused, but I could just do me. I had the freedom to express myself, but then still have a platform to release on. One thing that really binds all these labels together is that I've never really worked with people who weren't my friends. It's always been people who are sort of close to me. I think this is also because it's the way I started. Working together on something, it's about collaboration. So this is always what I'm looking for in other labels as well. Your Turkish heritage has influenced your approach to production, from your debut LP, Kıran, to this year's Ebedi EP. How has this developed over time? I think this comes down to rhythm as well. I’d say it's mostly influenced by my dad, who was a Turkish musician. He was coming from folk music, traditional Turkish music, and then transitioned to a more fusion sound when he moved to the Netherlands. He eventually formed a band with a jazz musician there. Aside from my heritage, I think this blend—classical Turkish music combined with the constant push for something new—has influenced me the most. My dad was always pushing boundaries in his music, trying new things, even if they didn't always work out. That mindset has definitely shaped me. For Turkish music especially, I feel it's about this approach to vibe and rhythms—a willingness to experiment and evolve.
translation.blurb: A masterclass in minimalism and percussion.
